text: Aristotle's ethical concept of the {{c1::Golden Mean}} (El Justo Medio) states that virtue is found by cultivating character traits that avoid the extremes of {{c1::excess}} and {{c1::deficiency}}.
explanation: Virtue is the balance point between two opposing vices. For example, Courage is the Golden Mean between Cowardice (deficiency of confidence) and Rashness (excess of confidence).
usage: To practice the Golden Mean, analyze your behaviors: do not act out of fear (deficiency) or act recklessly without thinking (excess), but act assertively and deliberately (mean).
spanish: El concepto ético de Aristóteles del Justo Medio establece que la virtud se encuentra al cultivar rasgos de carácter que evitan los extremos del exceso y la deficiencia.

text: The Socratic Method of cooperative argumentative dialogue is primarily used to stimulate critical thinking and expose contradictions in assumptions through {{c1::methodical questioning (Elenchus)}}.
explanation: Socrates did not lecture; instead, he asked questions that forced his interlocutors to clarify their definitions and recognize the logical limits or contradictions in their beliefs.
usage:
Example Question: What is justice?
  • The interlocutor provides a definition.
  • Socrates asks a counter-example question.
  • The interlocutor revises the definition.
spanish: El método socrático de diálogo argumentativo cooperativo se utiliza principalmente para estimular el pensamiento crítico y exponer contradicciones en los supuestos mediante preguntas metódicas (Elenchus).

scenario: Philosophy: Plato's Allegory of the Cave 🏛️
text: Plato's Allegory of the Cave illustrates that humans mistake sensory appearances for reality, and that true knowledge is obtained only by escaping the cave to perceive the {{c1::Theory of Forms (Mundo de las Ideas)}}.
explanation: The cave dwellers see only shadows cast on walls by a fire and believe the shadows are real objects. Escaping the cave represents the philosopher's journey of using reason to see the immutable, true essence (Forms) of reality.
usage: Core teaching: The physical world is a shadow/copy; the world of Ideas is the ultimate, immutable truth.
spanish: La alegoría de la cueva de Platón ilustra que los humanos confunden las apariencias sensoriales con la realidad, y que el conocimiento verdadero se obtiene únicamente al escapar de la cueva para percibir la Teoría de las Formas.

text: Early Greek philosophers sought the fundamental, originating substance of the cosmos, which they called the {{c1::Arche}}.
explanation: The Arche represents the primary material or source of all things. For Thales, the Arche was water; for Anaximenes, it was air; for Heraclitus, it was fire.
usage: The search for the Arche marks the birth of rational metaphysics, moving away from mythological explanations.
spanish: Los primeros filósofos griegos buscaron la sustancia fundamental y originaria del cosmos, a la que llamaron el Arjé (Arche).

text: Epicurus taught that the greatest good is to seek pleasure and avoid pain to achieve a state of mental tranquility known as {{c1::Ataraxia}}.
explanation: Ataraxia is a state of serene calmness and freedom from fear and worry, achieved by understanding the natural limits of desires and overcoming the fear of death.
usage: Ataraxia is contrasted with active, physical pleasure; it is the pleasure of absence of pain (aponia) and anxiety.
spanish: Epicuro enseñó que el mayor bien es buscar el placer y evitar el dolor para lograr un estado de tranquilidad mental conocido como ataraxia.

text: The philosophical school of {{c1::Cynicism}}, led by Diogenes of Sinope, advocated living in extreme simplicity and accordance with nature, rejecting social conventions.
explanation: Cynics believed that virtue is the only good, and that happiness is achieved by being self-sufficient and living naturally, free from desires for wealth, power, or fame.
usage: Diogenes famously lived in a tub on the streets of Athens, mocking social status and conventions.
spanish: La escuela filosófica del cinismo (Cynicism), liderada por Diógenes de Sinope, abogaba por vivir en extrema simplicidad y de acuerdo con la naturaleza, rechazando las convenciones sociales.

text: Skeptics argue that absolute knowledge is impossible, and that one should practice suspension of judgment, known as {{c1::Epoche}}.
explanation: Epoche is the suspension of belief or judgment regarding non-evident assertions. Pyrrhonian skeptics believed that by suspending judgment, one achieves tranquility (ataraxia) by stopping the search for dogmatic truths.
usage: Epoche is a method of questioning assumptions to prevent dogmatism.
spanish: Los escépticos argumentan que el conocimiento absoluto es imposible, y que uno debería practicar la suspensión del juicio, conocida como epojé (Epoche).

text: To construct logical deductive arguments, Aristotle developed the {{c1::syllogism}}, consisting of a major premise, minor premise, and a conclusion.
explanation: A syllogism is a form of reasoning where a conclusion is drawn from two assumed propositions. Example: 'All humans are mortal (major); Socrates is human (minor); therefore, Socrates is mortal (conclusion).'
usage: Syllogistic logic remained the dominant framework of formal logic in the West until the late 19th century.
spanish: Para construir argumentos deductivos lógicos, Aristóteles desarrolló el silogismo (syllogism), que consta de una premisa mayor, una premisa menor y una conclusión.

text: In the Republic, Plato proposes that the ideal state should be ruled by {{c1::philosopher kings}} who possess knowledge of the Good.
explanation: Plato argued that because philosophers have access to the immutable world of Forms (truth and justice), they are uniquely qualified to rule without greed or corruption, guiding society toward harmony.
usage: Plato's philosopher kings represent the rule of reason and intellect over physical desires and military power.
spanish: En la República, Platón propone que el estado ideal debería ser gobernado por reyes filósofos (philosopher kings) que poseen el conocimiento del Bien.

text: Socrates' ethical thesis that no one does evil knowingly, and that doing wrong is always a result of ignorance, is known as {{c1::virtue is knowledge}}.
explanation: This is Socratic intellectualism. Socrates believed that if a person truly understands what is good and just, they will naturally act virtuously. Acting maliciously is therefore a cognitive failure (ignorance).
usage: Under this view, rehabilitation and philosophical dialogue are preferred over retributive punishment.
spanish: La tesis ética de Sócrates de que nadie hace el mal a sabiendas, y que actuar mal es siempre resultado de la ignorancia, se conoce como la virtud es conocimiento (virtue is knowledge).
